title: What Is Asset Allocation? Definition, Formula & Examples
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: What is Asset Allocation? Learn the definition, formula, and real-world examples of Asset Allocation. Our comprehensive guide explains how Asset Allocation works and why it matters for investors.
excerpt: Asset allocation is the strategy of dividing your investment portfolio across different asset classes — primarily stocks, bonds, and cash equivalents — based on your financial goals, risk tolerance, a...

content.definition: Asset allocation is the strategy of dividing your investment portfolio across different asset classes — primarily stocks, bonds, and cash equivalents — based on your financial goals, risk tolerance, and time horizon. Research shows asset allocation, not individual stock selection, determines approximately 90% of portfolio returns over time. The right allocation balances growth potential with risk management, ensuring your portfolio aligns with your personal financial situation and objectives.
content.formula: Stock Allocation ≈ 110 − Your Age (rule of thumb); adjust based on risk tolerance and goals
content.example: A 35-year-old with moderate risk tolerance might allocate 75% stocks, 20% bonds, 5% cash. A 60-year-old nearing retirement might shift to 50% stocks, 40% bonds, 10% cash. Within stocks, diversify further: 60% U.S. large-cap, 15% U.S. small-cap, 25% international.
content.types: Strategic allocation sets long-term targets and rebalances periodically. Tactical allocation temporarily shifts weights based on market conditions. Dynamic allocation adjusts continuously as risk/return profiles change. Core-satellite allocation uses low-cost index funds as the core with active bets as satellites.
content.whyItMatters: Understanding Asset Allocation is essential for making informed investment decisions. Whether you are evaluating individual stocks, planning your portfolio allocation, or comparing investment options, Asset Allocation provides a quantitative framework that removes guesswork and helps you assess value objectively. Professional investors, analysts, and financial advisors all rely on Asset Allocation as a core metric in their decision-making process.
